title: Muhammad Ali Congressional Gold Medal Act
short_title: Muhammad Ali Congressional Gold Medal Act
plain_summary: This bill would give a special gold medal called a Congressional Gold Medal to Muhammad Ali after he died. The medal honors him for being a great boxer, fighting for civil rights, and helping people around the world. The medal would be given to his wife Lonnie Ali to keep. The government could also make bronze copies of the medal to sell to people who want to buy them. The money from selling these bronze medals would go back to the U.S. Mint to help pay for making the medals.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Muhammad Ali Congressional Gold Medal Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Findings
sections.text: Congress has found these facts about Muhammad Ali. He was born Cassius Marcellus Clay Junior on January seventeen nineteen forty two in Louisville Kentucky. His parents were Cassius Senior and Odessa Clay. Muhammad Ali was one of the most famous athletes of the twentieth century. He created some of the greatest sports memories in the United States. He won a gold medal at the nineteen sixty Summer Olympics and lit the Olympic torch at the nineteen ninety six Summer Olympics. After a great amateur career where he won two National AAU light heavyweight titles, Muhammad Ali became the first professional boxer ever to win the heavyweight title three separate times. On April twenty eight nineteen sixty seven the boxing authorities took away his title and banned him from boxing. This happened because he refused to fight in the Vietnam War for religious and moral reasons. However in nineteen seventy one the Supreme Court of the United States agreed with him. The court confirmed he was a conscientious objector (someone who refuses to fight in wars for moral reasons). His boxing license was given back to him and he was cleared of any wrongdoing. As an African American and a Muslim living during a time when his civil rights were questioned, Muhammad Ali fought against racism and religious discrimination. He became known as one of the champions of the Civil Rights Movement in the United States. Muhammad Ali received many awards for his boxing skills and his support of racial harmony. These awards included the Doctor Martin Luther King Memorial Award, the Spirit of America Award, the Amnesty International Lifetime Achievement Award, the Arthur Ashe Award for Courage, the Essence Living Legend Award, the Rainbow Coalition Lifetime Achievement Award, the XNBA Human Spirit Award, the Presidential Citizens Medal, and the Presidential Medal of Freedom. Many organizations recognized Muhammad Ali for his achievements both inside and outside the boxing ring. Sports Illustrated named him Sportsman of the Century. GQ magazine named him Athlete of the Century. The British Broadcasting Corporation named him Sports Personality of the Century. The Kentucky Athletic Hall of Fame named him Kentucky Athlete of the Century. The State of Kentucky named him Kentuckian of the Century. The Advertising Club of Louisville named him Louisvillian of the Century. The World Sports Awards of the Century named him Boxer of the Century. He was recognized by the International Boxing Hall of Fame and received honorary doctorate degrees from Muhlenberg College and Princeton University. Muhammad Ali received the prestigious Otto Hahn Peace Medal in Gold from the United Nations Association of Germany. This was for his work with the United Nations and the Civil Rights Movement in the United States. The California Bicentennial Foundation for the US Constitution chose Muhammad Ali for showing the importance of the Bill of Rights through his public activities. Even though Muhammad Ali was diagnosed with Parkinson's disease (a brain disorder that affects movement) in the early nineteen eighties, he dedicated his life to universal human rights and freedom. His commitment to equal justice and peace touched the lives of hundreds of thousands of people worldwide. President Jimmy Carter asked Muhammad Ali to meet with African leaders in Tanzania, Kenya, Nigeria, Liberia, and Senegal. This was part of President Carter's diplomatic efforts to promote human rights in the nineteen eighties. In nineteen ninety Muhammad Ali traveled to the Middle East to seek the release of United States and British hostages. These hostages were being held as human shields in the first Gulf War. Because of his intervention, fifteen United States hostages were freed on December second of that year. In nineteen ninety eight Muhammad Ali was chosen as the UN Messenger of Peace. Several Presidents of the United States recognized Muhammad Ali. President George W Bush on November seventeen two thousand two called him a man of peace and stated that across the world billions of people know Muhammad Ali as a brave compassionate and charming man and the American people are proud to call Muhammad Ali one of our own. President Bill Clinton stated that Muhammad Ali captured the world's imagination and its heart and that outside the ring Muhammad Ali has dedicated his life to working for children feeding the hungry supporting his faith and standing up for racial equality. He always fought for a just and more humane world breaking down barriers here in America and around the world. There is no telling how many tens of millions of people had their hearts swell with pride and their eyes swell with tears in nineteen ninety six when Muhammad Ali lit the Olympic torch because we know now and forever he is the greatest. President Jimmy Carter cited Muhammad Ali as Mister International Friendship. President Barack Obama as a Senator had a framed picture of Muhammad Ali hanging in his office and before announcing his intentions to run for President Obama visited with Muhammad Ali at the Ali Center in Louisville Kentucky. Muhammad Ali encouraged humanity through his perseverance and the support of thousands of people. He helped such organizations as the Chicago based adoption agency The Cradle, the Make A Wish Foundation, the Special Olympics organization, Best Buddies, and Herbert E Birch Services an organization that runs a school for handicapped children and young adults in addition to a summer camp for children with AIDS. Muhammad Ali and his wife Lonnie were the founding directors of the Muhammad Ali Parkinson Center in Phoenix Arizona and helped raise over fifty million dollars for Parkinson's research. The Center's mission is to provide excellence in treatment research and education for patients and families affected by Parkinson's disease and other movement disorders regardless of their ability to pay. Muhammad Ali was an inspiration to countless individuals with Parkinson's disease including members of the Rock Steady Boxing Foundation in Indianapolis Indiana which was founded to give people with Parkinson's disease hope by improving their quality of life using boxing for fitness. Muhammad Ali was one of the founding members of Athletes for Hope an organization created by a few very successful athletes of exemplary character who have a deep commitment to charitable and community causes. Muhammad Ali also established the Muhammad Ali Center in his hometown of Louisville Kentucky which promotes respect hope and understanding and inspires people everywhere to be as great as they can be. A visitor of the Muhammad Ali Center experiences the hows of Ali's life including how he found the courage dedication and discipline to become a world champion how he found the conviction to stand up for what he believed and how he turned his passion for excellence in the ring to a passion for peace on the world stage. Like Muhammad Ali himself the Muhammad Ali Center focuses on what brings individuals together not what sets them apart and is a global gathering place to which people can come both online and in person to learn share and celebrate our commonalities as human beings and to formulate ways of advancing humanity. Muhammad Ali helped to provide more than twenty two million aid packets to assist people in need and until the last years of his life he traveled on average more than two hundred days per year for humanitarian causes. Muhammad Ali known simply as the greatest transcended the glamour and glory of being a sports champion to become not only one of the greatest sports figures but one of the greatest role models of our time. On June third two thousand sixteen Muhammad Ali died at the age of seventy four.

sections.title: Section 3  Congressional Gold Medal
sections.text: The Speaker of the House of Representatives and the President pro tempore (temporary president) of the Senate shall make proper arrangements for giving a gold medal to Muhammad Ali on behalf of Congress. This medal will recognize his contributions to the United States. For this presentation the Secretary of the Treasury (called the Secretary in this law) shall create a gold medal with suitable symbols designs and words to be decided by the Secretary. After the gold medal is presented in honor of Muhammad Ali the medal shall be given to his wife Lonnie Ali.

sections.title: Section 4  Duplicate Medals
sections.text: The Secretary may create and sell copies of the gold medal made of bronze. These copies will be sold at a price that covers the cost of making the medals including labor materials dies use of machinery and overhead expenses.

sections.title: Section 5  Status of Medals
sections.text: Medals made under this law are national medals for purposes of chapter fifty one of title thirty one United States Code. For purposes of sections fifty one thirty four and fifty one thirty six of title thirty one United States Code all medals made under this law shall be considered to be numismatic items (collectible coins and medals).

sections.title: Section 6  Authority to Use Fund Amounts and Proceeds of Sale
sections.text: The government is authorized to charge the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund for whatever amounts are necessary to pay for the costs of the medals made under this law. Money received from selling duplicate bronze medals authorized under section four shall be put into the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund.
